blocks:
Ayahs

وَٱلسَّمَآءِ وَٱلطَّارِقِ (١)
By the heaven, and by the nightly visitor (1)
وَمَآ أَدْرَىٰكَ مَا ٱلطَّارِقُ (٢)
(and what will show you what is the nightly visitor? (2)
ٱلنَّجْمُ ٱلثَّاقِبُ (٣)
It is the brilliant star): (3)
إِن كُلُّ نَفْسٍ لَّمَّا عَلَيْهَا حَافِظٌ (٤)
there is a guard over every soul. (4)
فَلْيَنظُرِ ٱلْإِنسَـٰنُ مِمَّ خُلِقَ (٥)
So let man consider from what he was created. (5)
خُلِقَ مِن مَّآءٍ دَافِقٍ (٦)
He was created from an effusing fluid (6)
يَخْرُجُ مِنۢ بَيْنِ ٱلصُّلْبِ وَٱلتَّرَآئِبِ (٧)
which issues from between the loins and the breast-bones. (7)
إِنَّهُۥ عَلَىٰ رَجْعِهِۦ لَقَادِرٌ (٨)
Indeed He is able to bring him back [after death], (8)
يَوْمَ تُبْلَى ٱلسَّرَآئِرُ (٩)
on the day when the secrets are examined (9)
فَمَا لَهُۥ مِن قُوَّةٍ وَلَا نَاصِرٍ (١٠)
and he shall have neither power nor helper. (10)
وَٱلسَّمَآءِ ذَاتِ ٱلرَّجْعِ (١١)
By the resurgent heaven, (11)
وَٱلْأَرْضِ ذَاتِ ٱلصَّدْعِ (١٢)
and by the furrowed earth: (12)
إِنَّهُۥ لَقَوْلٌ فَصْلٌ (١٣)
it is indeed a conclusive discourse, (13)
وَمَا هُوَ بِٱلْهَزْلِ (١٤)
and not a jest. (14)
إِنَّهُمْ يَكِيدُونَ كَيْدًا (١٥)
Indeed they are devising a stratagem, (15)
وَأَكِيدُ كَيْدًا (١٦)
and I [too] am devising a plan. (16)
فَمَهِّلِ ٱلْكَـٰفِرِينَ أَمْهِلْهُمْ رُوَيْدًۢا (١٧)
So respite the faithless; give them a gentle respite. (17)

(Commentary)

The Surah contains a warning regarding the Return (المعاد) — the restoration of life after death for judgment — and establishes proof for it through the absolute power (إطلاق القدرة) of Allah. It emphasizes this discourse, alludes to the reality of that Day, and concludes with a threat directed at the disbelievers.

The Surah is Meccan in its context (سياق).

His saying, the Exalted: وَالسَّماءِ وَالطَّارِقِ وَما أَدْراكَ مَا الطَّارِقُ النَّجْمُ الثَّاقِبُ By the sky and the Night-visitant (1) And what will show you what the Night-visitant is? (2) It is the brilliant star (3). The word al-tarq (الطرق) originally—according to what has been said—means striking with force such that a sound is heard; from it are derived the words "hammer" (المطرقة) and "path" (الطريق), because travelers strike it with their feet. Later, its usage became common for traveling a path, and then it became specific to arriving by night, because one who arrives at night usually finds the doors closed and thus knocks (يطرق) or strikes them. Subsequently, the "night-visitant" (الطارق) became a common term for everything that appears at night. What is meant by the "night-visitant" in the verse is the star that rises at night.

The word al-thaqb (الثقب) originally means piercing (الخرق), then it came to mean the luminous, shining light because it pierces the darkness with its radiance. It also carries the meaning of loftiness and elevation; from this is said "the bird pierced" (ثقب الطائر), meaning it rose and ascended as if it were piercing the atmosphere with its flight.

Thus, His saying: وَالسَّماءِ وَالطَّارِقِ (By the sky and the Night-visitant) is an oath by the heaven and the star that rises at night. His saying: وَما أَدْراكَ مَا الطَّارِقُ (And what will show you what the Night-visitant is?) is to magnify the status of that by which the oath is taken, which is the night-visitant. His saying: النَّجْمُ الثَّاقِبُ (It is the brilliant star) is an explanation (بيان) of the night-visitant. The sentence functions as the answer to an implied question; it is as if when it was said, "And what will show you what the night-visitant is?" a question was asked: "So what is the night-visitant?" and it was answered: "The brilliant star."

His saying, the Exalted: إِنْ كُلُّ نَفْسٍ لَمَّا عَلَيْها حافِظٌ There is no soul but has a keeper over it (4). This is the response to the oath (جواب القسم). The word lamma (لما) here means "except" (إلا). The meaning is: there is no soul but that there is a keeper over it. What is meant by the keeper standing over its protection is the recording of its good and evil deeds as they proceed from it, so that it may be held accountable for them on the Day of Resurrection and be recompensed accordingly. Thus, the keeper (الحافظ) is the angel, and the kept (المحفوظ) is the deed, as the Exalted said: وَإِنَّ عَلَيْكُمْ لَحافِظِينَ كِراماً كاتِبِينَ يَعْلَمُونَ ما تَفْعَلُونَ Indeed, there are over you watchers, (10) noble writers, (11) who know whatever you do (12) (al-Infitar: 10-12).

It is not improbable that what is meant by the keeping of the soul is the preservation of its essence (ذات) as well as its deeds. The "keeper" would then refer to the genus of keepers, implying that souls are preserved and do not perish with death, nor do they decay; rather, when Allah gives life to the bodies, He returns the souls to them. Thus, the human being remains the exact same worldly human being in his identity (عينية) and personhood (شخصه), and He then recompenses him according to what his preserved deeds of good or evil necessitate.

This is supported by many verses indicating the preservation of things, such as His saying, the Exalted: قُلْ يَتَوَفَّاكُمْ مَلَكُ الْمَوْتِ الَّذِي وُكِّلَ بِكُمْ Say, ‘The angel of death, who has been given charge of you, will take your souls’ (al-Sajdah: 11), and His saying: اللهُ يَتَوَفَّى الْأَنْفُسَ حِينَ مَوْتِها وَالَّتِي لَمْ تَمُتْ فِي مَنامِها فَيُمْسِكُ الَّتِي قَضى عَلَيْهَا الْمَوْتَ Allah takes the souls at the time of their death, and those who have not died, in their sleep. Then He retains those for whom He has ordained death (al-Zumar: 42).

This interpretation does not contradict the apparent meaning of the previously mentioned verse from Surah al-Infitar—that the preservation by the angels is the recording—for the preservation of the human soul itself is also part of the "recording" (الكتابة), as inferred from His saying: إِنَّا كُنَّا نَسْتَنْسِخُ ما كُنْتُمْ تَعْمَلُونَ Indeed, We used to record what you used to do (al-Jathiyah: 29), as has been alluded to previously.

Through this interpretation, the objection against using absolute power (إطلاق القدرة) as a proof for the Return is repelled, as will follow. The gist of the objection is that absolute power is only effective regarding what is possible (الممكن), but the restoration of the human being in his exact identity is impossible; for the human created a second time is merely a "like" (مثل) of the worldly human created first, not the same person (شخص) who was created first, and a "like" of a thing is other than the thing itself, not its very essence (عينه).

The manner in which this is repelled is that the personhood (شخصية) of a human individual is defined by his soul (النفس), not by his body. Since the soul is preserved (محفوظة), when the body is created and the soul is attached to it, he is the worldly human being in his personhood, even if the body—in comparison to the [first] body and disregarding the soul—is a "like" and not the exact same entity (عيناً).

His saying, the Exalted: فَلْيَنْظُرِ الْإِنْسانُ مِمَّ خُلِقَ So let man observe from what he was created (5). That is, what is the origin of his creation? And what is it that Allah transformed into a human being?

The sentence is branched (متفرعة) from the preceding verse and what it indicates by its implication (فحواها) according to the context. The sum of the meaning is: since every soul is preserved in its essence and its deeds without perishing or its deeds being forgotten, let man submit (فليذعن) to the fact that he will return to his Lord and be recompensed for what he has done. He should not find this far-fetched; rather, to achieve this submission, he should look at the origin of his creation and remember that he was created from a gushing water that issues from between the loins and the breastbones.

Thus, He who initiated his creation from water of this description is surely capable of his return and his revival after death.

The use of the word خُلِقَ (was created) in the passive voice (مبنياً للمفعول), omitting the mention of the Agent (الفاعل)—who is Allah, the Glorified—is an allusion to the manifest nature of His command. Similar to this is His saying: خُلِقَ مِنْ ماءٍ (He was created from water), etc.

His saying, the Exalted: خُلِقَ مِنْ ماءٍ دافِقٍ He was created from a gushing water (6). Gushing (الدفق) is the pouring out and flowing of water with force and speed; the "gushing water" is the semen. The sentence is an answer to an implied question suggested by His saying: مِمَّ خُلِقَ (from what he was created).

His saying, the Exalted: يَخْرُجُ مِنْ بَيْنِ الصُّلْبِ وَالتَّرائِبِ emerging from between the loins and the breastbones (7). The sulb (الصلب) is the back, and the tara'ib (الترائب) is the plural of taribah (تريبة), which is the chest bone.

The opinions [of the commentators] regarding this verse and what precedes it have differed in a strange manner. What is apparent is that what is meant by بَيْنِ الصُّلْبِ وَالتَّرائِبِ (between the loins and the breastbones) is that specific part of the body enclosed between the walls of the backbones and the chest bones (1).

His saying, the Exalted: إِنَّهُ عَلى رَجْعِهِ لَقادِرٌ He is indeed able to bring him back (8). The "return" (الرجع) is the restoration (الإعادة). The pronoun in إِنَّهُ (He) refers to Allah, the Exalted. He sufficed with a pronoun despite the context being one for a manifest noun (إظهار) because of His obviousness, similar to His saying خُلِقَ (was created) in the passive voice.

The meaning is that He who created man from water of that description is surely capable of his restoration and revival after death—and his restoration is like his beginning—because power over a thing is power over its "like" (مثله), as the ruling for likes (الأمثال) regarding what is possible and what is impossible is one and the same.

His saying, the Exalted: يَوْمَ تُبْلَى السَّرائِرُ on the day when the secrets are examined (9). This is a temporal adverb (ظرف) for the "return" (الرجع). The "secret" (السريرة) is what a person keeps secret and hides within himself. al-Bala' (البلاء) is testing (الاختبار), identifying, and scrutinizing.

The meaning is: the day when what man has hidden and kept secret of beliefs and the effects of deeds—their good and their evil—is tested, such that the good is distinguished from the evil and man is recompensed for it. Thus, the verse is similar in meaning to His saying, the Exalted: إِنْ تُبْدُوا ما فِي أَنْفُسِكُمْ أَوْ تُخْفُوهُ يُحاسِبْكُمْ بِهِ اللهُ Whether you reveal what is in your hearts or hide it, Allah will bring you to account for it (al-Baqarah: 284).

His saying, the Exalted: فَما لَهُ مِنْ قُوَّةٍ وَلا ناصِرٍ then he will have no power or helper (10). That is, he will have no power within himself to protect himself from the punishment of Allah, nor any helper to ward that off from him. This means there is no power there to repel evil from him, neither from himself nor from anyone else.

His saying, the Exalted: وَالسَّماءِ ذاتِ الرَّجْعِ وَالْأَرْضِ ذاتِ الصَّدْعِ By the sky with its returning [rain], (11) and by the earth with its splitting [with herbage] (12). This is an oath after an oath to emphasize the matter of the Resurrection and the return to Allah.

What is meant by the sky being "possessing return" (ذات رجع) is what appears to the senses of its movement through the rising of the stars after their setting and their setting after their rising. It is also said: its "return" is its rains. What is meant by the earth being "possessing splitting" (ذات صدع) is its cracking and splitting open with vegetation. The suitability of these two oaths to that which is sworn upon—the return after death and the emergence from the graves—is manifest.

His saying, the Exalted: إِنَّهُ لَقَوْلٌ فَصْلٌ وَما هُوَ بِالْهَزْلِ it is indeed a decisive word, (13) and it is not a jest (14). al-Fasl (الفصل) is the separation of one thing from another until there is a gap between them. The expression "decisive" (فصل)—while meaning "that which decides" (فاصل)—is for emphasis (مبالغة), like saying "Zayd is justice" (زيد عدل). al-Hazl (الهزل) is the opposite of seriousness (الجد).

These two verses are the response to the oath. The pronoun in إِنَّهُ (it) refers to the Quran. The meaning is: I swear by such-and-forth that the Quran is a word that decides between truth and falsehood, and it is not a speech in which there is no seriousness. Thus, what it validates is a truth in which there is no doubt, and what it invalidates is a falsehood in which there is no doubt. Therefore, what it informs you of regarding the Resurrection and the Return is a truth in which there is no doubt.

It is also said: the pronoun refers to the previously mentioned news of the Return and the Resurrection, but the former view is more appropriate.

His saying, the Exalted: إِنَّهُمْ يَكِيدُونَ كَيْداً وَأَكِيدُ كَيْداً Indeed they are devising a stratagem, (15) and I [too] am devising a stratagem (16). That is, the disbelievers are plotting through their disbelief and their denial of the Return—a plot by which they intend to extinguish the light of Allah and invalidate your call. And I plot against them through their very deeds by way of gradual leading (الاستدراج), granting respite (الإملاء), and misguiding by sealing their hearts and placing a veil over their hearing and their sight—a plot by which I drive them toward the punishment of the Day of Resurrection.

His saying, the Exalted: فَمَهِّلِ الْكافِرِينَ أَمْهِلْهُمْ رُوَيْداً So respite the faithless; give them a brief respite (17). al-Tamhil (التمهيل) and al-Imhal (الإمهال) carry the same meaning, except that the form taf'il (تفعيل) implies gradualness (التدريج) while if'al (إفعال) implies suddenness (الدفعة). al-Ruwayd (الرويد) means "a little."

The meaning is: since there is a plot from them and a plot from Me against them through the very thing they plot with—and Allah is dominant over His affair—then wait for them and do not hasten against them; wait for them a little, for what He has promised them will surely come to them, and everything that is coming is near.

In the expression first with مَهِّلِ (respite), which suggests gradualness, and then—coupled with the restriction of رُوَيْداً (briefly)—with أَمْهِلْهُمْ (give them respite), which suggests suddenness, there is a manifest subtlety.

(Narrative Discussion)

In Tafsir al-Qummi, regarding His saying, the Exalted: إِنْ كُلُّ نَفْسٍ لَمَّا عَلَيْها حافِظٌ (There is no soul but has a keeper over it), he said: "The angels."

And in it, regarding His saying, the Exalted: خُلِقَ مِنْ ماءٍ دافِقٍ (He was created from a gushing water), he said: "The semen (النطفة) that comes out with force."

And in it, regarding His saying, the Exalted: يَخْرُجُ مِنْ بَيْنِ الصُّلْبِ وَالتَّرائِبِ (emerging from between the loins and the breastbones), he said: "The sulb is the man, and the tara'ib is the woman, which is her chest."

I say: The narration, despite being unattributed (إضمار) and disconnected (إرسال), is not without some issues.

And in it, regarding His saying, the Exalted: يَوْمَ تُبْلَى السَّرائِرُ (on the day when the secrets are examined), he said: "They are uncovered."

In al-Majma', it is narrated in a raised (مرفوعاً) report from Abu al-Darda who said: "The Messenger of Allah صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله said: 'Allah has entrusted His creation with four traits: prayer, zakat, the fast of the month of Ramadan, and the major ritual bath (الغسل) from sexual impurity (الجنابة); these are the secrets (السرائر) of which Allah the Exalted said: on the day when the secrets are examined.'"

I say: Perhaps this is of the nature of mentioning some instances (المصاديق), as supported by the following narration.

In it, from Mu'adh ibn Jabal who said: "I asked the Messenger of Allah صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله: 'What are these secrets (السرائر) with which Allah will test the servants in the Hereafter?' He replied: 'Your secrets are your deeds: prayer, fasting, zakat, ablution (الوضوء), the major ritual bath from sexual impurity, and every obligatory duty—for all deeds are hidden secrets; if a man wishes, he says: "I have prayed," while he has not prayed, and if he wishes, he says: "I have performed ablution," while he has not performed ablution—that is His saying: on the day when the secrets are examined.'"

In Tafsir al-Qummi, regarding His saying, the Exalted: فَما لَهُ مِنْ قُوَّةٍ وَلا ناصِرٍ (then he will have no power or helper), he said: "He has no power with which to overcome his Creator, nor any helper from Allah to help him if He intends evil for him."

And in it, regarding His saying, the Exalted: وَالسَّماءِ ذاتِ الرَّجْعِ (By the sky with its returning [rain]), he said: "Possessing rain," and وَالْأَرْضِ ذاتِ الصَّدْعِ (and by the earth with its splitting), he said: "Possessing vegetation."

In al-Majma': إِنَّهُ لَقَوْلٌ فَصْلٌ (it is indeed a decisive word) means that the Quran decides between truth and falsehood by clarifying each of them. This has been narrated from Imam al-Sadiq عليه‌السلام.

In al-Durr al-Manthur, Ibn Abi Shaybah, al-Darimi, al-Tirmidhi, Muhammad ibn Nasr, and Ibn al-Anbari in al-Masahif narrated from al-Harith al-A'war who said: "I entered the mosque and found that the people had become engrossed in [idle] talk. So I went to Ali and informed him. He said: 'Have they really done it?' I heard the Messenger of Allah صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله saying: 'Indeed, there will be a trial (فتنة).' I asked: 'What is the way out of it, O Messenger of Allah?' He said: 'The Book of Allah; in it is the news of those before you, the report of those after you, and the judgment for what is between you. It is the decisive word (الفصل), not a jest (الهزل). Whoever among the tyrants abandons it, Allah will break him; whoever seeks guidance (الهوى) in other than it, Allah will misguide him. It is the firm rope of Allah, it is the wise reminder (الذكر الحكيم), and it is the straight path (الصراط المستقيم). It is that by which desires do not deviate, nor are the scholars ever satiated by it, nor do tongues become confused by it, nor does it wear out from repetition, nor do its wonders ever cease. It is that which the jinn did not cease [listening to] when they heard it until they said: إِنَّا سَمِعْنا قُرْآناً عَجَباً يَهْدِي إِلَى الرُّشْدِ (Indeed we have heard a wonderful Quran, (1) which guides to rectitude). Whoever speaks by it has spoken the truth, whoever judges by it has acted justly, whoever acts upon it is rewarded, and whoever calls to it is guided to a straight path.'"

I say: Something similar has been narrated from Mu'adh ibn Jabal from him صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله, and it was narrated in a concise form by Ibn Mardawayh from Ali عليه‌السلام.
